chunks.text: So last time, last week, we talked about how we looked at the search problem that we talked

chunks.text: in faster than log n time. But luckily, we are in a model of computation which has a

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: stronger operation, namely random accessing.

chunks.text: And if we stored the things that we were looking for, we have unique keys, and those keys are

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: integers. Then if I have an item with key k, if I store it at index k in my array, then

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I can find it and manipulate it in constant time. That's pretty cool. That's what we call

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: the direct access array.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: it when we're talking about sequences is we are giving kind of extrinsic semantics

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to the slots where we are storing these things. Basically, I could put any item in any slot.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Where it was in my array had nothing to do with what those things were. Here, we are

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: using intrinsic semantics on my array that if I have an item with k k, it must be at

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: index k. That's the thing that we're taking advantage of here. And then we can use this

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: nice, powerful linear branching random access operation to find that thing in constant time

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: because that's our model of computation.
